accomplishments: Create bullet-point lists for your hobbies
Build a navigation menu with lists

do.title: Make a bullet-point list
steps: Replace these reasons with YOUR actual reasons
do.code:
Why I'm Learning to Code

  • I want to build my own projects
  • I'm tired of paying for simple websites
  • I want a career change
  • I love solving puzzles
do.result: You'll see bullet points (•) next to each reason!
do.proofRequired: your bulleted list with YOUR reasons

concepts.name: The <ul> tag (unordered list)
concepts.definition: Creates bullet-point lists. 'Unordered' means the sequence doesn't matter — you could rearrange items and the meaning stays the same.
concepts.analogy: A grocery list. Order doesn't matter — you could buy milk before eggs or eggs before milk.

concepts.name: The <ol> tag (ordered list)
concepts.definition: Creates numbered lists. 'Ordered' means sequence MATTERS — step 1 must come before step 2.
concepts.analogy: A recipe. You can't frost a cake before baking it — order matters!

concepts.name: The <li> tag (list item)
concepts.definition: Each <li> represents ONE item in the list. Works inside both <ul> and <ol>.
concepts.analogy: Individual items on your shopping list or steps in your recipe.

understand.keyTakeaway: <ul> + <li> = bullet-point list for items in any order. <ol> + <li> = numbered list for sequential steps.

build.goal: Create step-by-step instructions using numbers instead of bullets

codeComparison.new:
How to Make Coffee (My Way)

  1. Grind fresh coffee beans
  2. Boil water to 200°F
  3. Pour water over grounds
  4. Wait 4 minutes
  5. Press plunger slowly
  6. Pour and enjoy!

build.hint: The browser automatically numbers your steps: 1, 2, 3, 4... You don't have to type the numbers!

challenge.title: Build a Skills & Resources Page
challenge.mission: Create a page that lists your current skills, learning goals, and helpful resources — organized with multiple list types.
requirements: Create skills.html
Use <ul> for your current skills (3+ items)
Use <ol> for your learning roadmap (5+ steps in order)
Use nested lists for at least one section
Add a resources section with links to helpful sites
Include <nav> with list-based navigation
challenge.starterCode:
My Development Journey

  • Home

Skills I'm Building

My Learning Roadmap
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a 'Wins This Week' section with an <ol> listing 3-5 coding accomplishments (even tiny ones count!)

capabilities: Build navigation menus using list structure
Nest lists inside other lists for sub-items
Choose the right list type for your content
Understand why lists matter for screen readers

bridge.preview: In Lesson 5, you'll build interactive forms — text inputs, dropdowns, checkboxes, and buttons that collect information from users.

accomplishments: Create a table with rows and columns
Add headers that describe your data

do.title: Make your first table
steps: Save and open in your browser
do.code:
My Learning Schedule

Monday HTML practice 1 hour
Tuesday Build project 2 hours
Wednesday Watch tutorials 1 hour
do.result: You'll see a grid with 3 rows and 3 columns, with borders around each cell!
do.proofRequired: your table with borders and data

concepts.name: The <table> tag
concepts.definition: Creates the container for your entire table. Everything else goes inside it.
concepts.analogy: The frame of a spreadsheet or Excel grid.

concepts.name: The <tr> tag (table row)
concepts.definition: Each <tr> is ONE row going across the table horizontally. Tables are built row-by-row.
concepts.analogy: Like horizontal lines on notebook paper - each line is a row.

concepts.name: The <td> tag (table data)
concepts.definition: Each <td> is ONE cell in the row. 'td' stands for 'table data' - it's where your actual content goes.
concepts.analogy: Individual boxes in the grid where you write information.

concepts.name: The <th> tag (table header)
concepts.definition: Like <td> but for headers. Browsers automatically make it bold and centered. Screen readers announce these as headers.
concepts.analogy: The column titles in a spreadsheet - the gray row at the top that labels what each column contains.

understand.keyTakeaway: Tables are built row-by-row. Each <tr> creates a new row. Each <td> inside that row creates a cell.

build.goal: Add table headers to make the first row stand out
build.hint: The first row is now bold and centered automatically because you used <th> instead of <td>

challenge.title: Build a Pricing Comparison Table
challenge.mission: Create a comparison table showing different plans, features, or options side-by-side.
requirements: Create comparison.html
Use <thead> and <tbody>
At least 3 columns comparing different options
At least 5 rows showing different features/criteria
Use <th> for both column headers AND row headers
Add navigation linking back to your other pages
challenge.starterCode:
Where Should I Learn?

Platform Cost Best For My Rating
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a <tfoot> section at the bottom with a total or summary row

capabilities: Add headers with <th> that are bold and centered
Display data in rows and columns
Understand when tables are appropriate (data only!)

bridge.preview: In Lesson 7, you'll learn Semantic HTML — the difference between beginner code and professional code. You'll use tags like <header>, <nav>, <main>, <section>, <article>, and <footer>.

accomplishments: Create a text input where users can type
Build dropdown menus with multiple choices

do.title: Create your first text input
steps: Save and open in your browser
Click in the input box and type your name
do.code: Get in Touch
do.result: You'll see a text box where you can type! The label 'Your Name:' sits next to it.
do.proofRequired: the input box with text typed inside it

concepts.name: The <form> tag
concepts.definition: A container for all input elements. Think of it as a clipboard that holds all the fields you want someone to fill out.
concepts.analogy: A clipboard with a form. The <form> tag is the clipboard, labels are printed questions, inputs are blank lines to write on.

concepts.name: The <label> tag
concepts.definition: Text that describes what the input is for. It's clickable — when you click the label, it focuses the input!
concepts.analogy: The question on a form that tells you what to write in each blank.

concepts.name: The <input> tag
concepts.definition: Creates an input field. It's self-closing (no </input> needed). The 'type' attribute changes what kind of input it is.
concepts.analogy: Different types of blanks on a form: short line for name, box for checkmark, circle for radio button.

concepts.name: The for/id connection
concepts.definition: The label's 'for' attribute must match the input's 'id' attribute. This connection is REQUIRED for accessibility.
concepts.analogy: Like matching question numbers on a test - question #1 goes with answer box #1.

concepts.name: Radio vs Checkbox
concepts.definition: Radio buttons: only one choice allowed (same 'name'). Checkboxes: multiple choices allowed.
concepts.analogy: Radio = multiple choice test question. Checkbox = 'select all that apply' question.

concepts.name: The <select> tag
concepts.definition: Creates a dropdown menu. Each <option> inside represents one choice in the menu.
concepts.analogy: Like a dropdown menu on a phone - tap it and options appear.

understand.keyTakeaway: Every input needs a label. Connect them with matching 'for' and 'id' attributes.

build.goal: Add an email field and a message box to your form
build.hint: The email input will validate that it looks like an email address when you submit!

challenge.title: Build a Complete Signup Form
challenge.mission: Create a realistic user registration form with validation.
requirements: Create signup.html
Include: username, email, password fields
Add a 'Confirm Password' field
Use a dropdown for 'How did you hear about us?'
Add checkboxes for 'I agree to Terms of Service'
Use the 'required' attribute on at least 3 fields
Add 'placeholder' attributes to show example text
challenge.starterCode: Create Your Account
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a 'Date of Birth' field with type="date" and a phone number field with type="tel"

capabilities: Make radio buttons for single choices
Connect labels to inputs with 'for' and 'id'
Make fields required with the 'required' attribute

bridge.preview: In Lesson 6, you'll organize data into tables — rows, columns, headers, and structured information display.

accomplishments: Embed a video that plays in the browser
Add audio files that users can play
Embed YouTube videos on your site
Understand when to use each media type

do.title: Add a video to your page
steps: Save and open in your browser
do.code:
My Media Gallery

Sample Video

Try clicking play! You can pause, adjust volume, and go fullscreen.
do.result: You'll see a video player with play/pause controls, a timeline, and volume controls!

concepts.name: The <video> tag
concepts.definition: Embeds a video file that plays directly in the browser (no YouTube, no external player needed). The 'controls' attribute shows play/pause buttons.
concepts.analogy: Like a DVD player built into your webpage - plays video right there without opening another app.

concepts.name: The <audio> tag
concepts.definition: Works exactly like <video> but for sound only. Shows a smaller audio player with play/pause and volume.
concepts.analogy: Like a music player widget - plays sound files without needing a separate music app.

concepts.name: The <iframe> tag
concepts.definition: Embeds another website INSIDE your page. Like a window showing another site. Used for YouTube, Google Maps, CodePen demos.
concepts.analogy: Picture-in-picture TV showing another channel - a window to another website inside yours.

concepts.name: Video/Audio attributes
concepts.definition: controls (show buttons), autoplay (start automatically - annoying!), loop (repeat forever), muted (start silent), poster (thumbnail image).
concepts.analogy: Settings on a TV remote: volume, channel, play/pause, mute.

understand.keyTakeaway: <video controls> adds a video player. <audio controls> adds audio. <iframe> embeds other websites. Always include 'controls' attribute!

build.goal: Add audio and a YouTube video to your page

codeComparison.new:
Sample Audio

My Favorite Tutorial

build.hint: Get YouTube embed code: go to any video, click Share → Embed, copy the <iframe> code

challenge.title: Build a Multimedia Portfolio Page
challenge.mission: Create a page showcasing media — videos about your interests, favorite music, embedded content.
requirements: Create multimedia.html
Add at least one <video> element (can use sample videos or your own)
Add at least one <audio> element
Embed at least one YouTube video relevant to your interests
Embed a Google Map of a meaningful location
Use semantic HTML structure (<header>, <main>, <section>, etc.)
Add descriptions/captions for each media item
Include navigation back to your other pages
challenge.starterCode:
Things I Love

A multimedia showcase

  • Home
  • About
  • Media

© 2024 Your Name
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add 'poster' attribute to your video (thumbnail image before playing) and create a video with 'loop' and 'muted' for background ambiance

capabilities: Use controls, autoplay, loop, and muted attributes
Add Google Maps to your pages
Provide fallback content for unsupported browsers
Choose appropriate media formats (mp4, mp3, etc.)

bridge.preview: In Lesson 10, you'll learn to use Developer Tools to inspect, debug, and understand your HTML. This is THE skill that separates beginners from professionals.
